Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine. I would tell myself I'd study after dinner or on weekends, but it rarely happened the way I planned. Most of the time I'd just scroll through my phone or hang out with friends from my extracurricular activities instead. When I did study, it was usually late at night when the anxiety got too bad to ignore, and I'd just skim through whatever seemed most urgent.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without opening anything course-related, then panic when a deadline was close and try to cram everything at once. It never felt like I had control over when I studied—it was more like the deadlines controlled me. Sometimes I'd miss deadlines entirely because I just couldn't bring myself to start.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or panic that made me log in. Like, I'd see a notification or remember that something was due, and that sick feeling in my stomach would push me to at least open the LMS. Sometimes I logged in just to see how much I'd missed, which usually made me feel worse. It was rarely because I actually wanted to learn something.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple of weeks early in the semester where I tried to check in regularly, but it never stuck. Life stuff kept getting in the way, and the more I fell behind, the harder it was to even look at the LMS. It became this thing I dreaded rather than something routine.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I learn better when I can see things visually. I could pause and rewatch parts I didn't understand. But even then, I watched a lot of video content without really absorbing it because my mind was elsewhere. At least with videos I didn't have to read walls of text.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums completely—never posted anything. They felt awkward and I didn't know what to say that wouldn't sound stupid. I also skipped most of the readings and supplementary materials because there was just so much, and I didn't have the energy to sort through what was actually important.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly tied to passing the course, like if it was graded. Even then, I'd weigh whether the points were worth the effort. If something seemed too complicated or time-consuming for what it was worth grade-wise, I'd skip it. I know that's not the best approach, but I was just trying to survive.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they didn't exist to me. If something wasn't required, my brain automatically filed it under 'not my problem.' Required ones at least had some urgency attached, even if I still struggled to complete them. The optional stuff felt like extra weight I couldn't carry.
Q9: Yeah, sometimes I'd download lecture slides or PDFs and look at them on my phone when I had time, like during commutes or waiting around. I also borrowed notes from a classmate a few times. Those moments of studying wouldn't show up anywhere in the system, but they happened.
Q10: There were times I'd sit with my notes or downloaded materials and try to make sense of concepts, but I wasn't logged into anything. Or I'd think about course content while doing other things, trying to understand it in my head. The LMS only sees clicks and logins, not the mental effort happening outside of it.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a video or save some slides, then go through them later without being connected. With limited resources, I couldn't always have reliable internet, so offline studying was sometimes my only option. But the system would just show that one download, not the hours I might've spent with it afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart for me. I was dealing with a lot of stress and anxiety—like, really bad anxiety that made it hard to focus on anything. My attendance dropped, I couldn't bring myself to complete assignments, and I felt like I was drowning. The sadness just made everything feel pointless.
Q13: Absolutely. I was juggling extracurriculars, family stuff because we don't have a lot of money, and this constant exhaustion. When I was confused by the material, I didn't have the energy to figure it out. The fatigue made even simple tasks feel impossible. The LMS became another source of stress rather than help.
Q14: All the time. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through materials without actually reading them, just going through the motions. It was like my body was there but my brain had checked out. I was too anxious to focus properly.
Q15: I postponed almost everything important. Even when I knew an assignment was due, I'd avoid it because starting felt overwhelming. The anxiety would build up, and then I'd feel paralyzed. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would come and I still couldn't face it. It's a terrible cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that sometimes got me moving, but often too late. I'd see a deadline approaching and feel this rush of panic, then either scramble to do something half-done or just accept that I'd missed it. The deadlines didn't motivate me in a healthy way—they just added to my stress.
Q17: I didn't get much feedback because I barely submitted anything. The few times I did get feedback, it was usually negative, which made me want to engage even less. It felt like confirmation that I was failing, so why bother trying? I know feedback is supposed to help, but it just made me feel worse about myself.
Q18: The course had so many modules and activities that it felt overwhelming from the start. With 18 online courses, I couldn't keep track of everything. The structure assumed I had more time and focus than I actually did. I needed something simpler, more guided, but instead it felt like being thrown into the deep end.
Q19: The sheer amount of content made engagement harder. There was no clear path through it all, so I never knew where to start. Having visual materials helped when I could focus, but the lack of structure made me feel lost. If there had been clearer priorities or smaller chunks, maybe I could've kept up.
Q20: I think that was me sometimes. I'd log in, click around, watch some videos, but never actually complete or submit anything. It's like you're trying to engage but you can't follow through. The activity is there because you're attempting something, but the mental block stops you from finishing. It looks productive but it's really just struggling.
Q21: That wasn't my experience at all. My low activity matched my poor performance pretty accurately. But I guess some people are just more efficient or study offline more effectively. They might not need the LMS as much if they have other resources or better study skills than me.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked things and how long you were supposedly on a page. But they can't show if you were actually paying attention or just had it open while staring at the ceiling. They miss the emotional state behind the activity—whether someone is anxious, sad, or checked out. Numbers don't capture struggle.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling like you can keep up. It's not just clicking through materials—it's understanding them and wanting to understand more. I wasn't engaged this semester. I was just trying to survive, and there's a big difference between those two things.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was battling with my mental health. The anxiety and sadness that made it hard to even get out of bed some days—none of that shows up in login data. It also wouldn't show the mindfulness exercises I started doing to cope, which actually helped my mood even if they didn't help my grades. My engagement was low, but my struggle was high.
